Correction on my previous comment: the otPlatAlarmMicroStartAt() does not add a time to the timer/scheduler, but I see now that it forces the next sleep/alarm event to be triggered at the specified time.  Simon had a comment before ([here](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/pull/7500#discussion_r950271013)) that only the Timer module is supposed to call this function. Basically it's the Timer::Scheduler::SetAlarm() method that sets the timer alarm of the simulation platform timer to the time of the first-to-occur timer event  in a list of events that it maintains. So the single 'alarm' value ought to be under control of the Timer module. Multiple processes may register an event with the Timer, and the Timer sorts these values so that a wake-up of the node can occur at the time of the earliest of these events. For this reason forcing the alarm value to some value seems potentially risky because scheduled tasks could be running late.

On the other hand this gives exactly the behavior of a blocking radio driver (that also forces some tasks to be run potentially late.) that postpones all remaining OT tasks until after the time that the Tx is done.

I haven't had much time to look into this, but i recently ran across  a funky edge case where I'm concerned that input managers don't cover the same functionality as root input managers. here's a (somewhat) trivial run down:

if we have op_a return 2.  op_b takes it as input, adds 1 and returns 
if we use a root input manager to provide 3 as input to op_b and just execute op_b we get 4 as expected. 

the typical case for using an input manager is to load the input to an op in a special way (ie output from an op is a dataframe, but we want it in a different format for the next op). 
if we have an input manager on op_b that multiplies the output by 2, we execute the job and get 5 as expected. 

the other use case for input managers is to replace root input managers
if we have a new input manager that provides 3 as input to op_b and we execute op_b we get 4 as expected. BUT if we execute the full job, the input manager will still provide 3 to op_b. this is because there isn't a good way to determine which type of input manager (the first version or the root manager replacing version) is intended

the way we get around this is by writing logic in the input manager to check if there is an upstream output, and if so we handle it differently (the example in the docs is to make the input manager extend from an IO manager and then call the IO manager's load_input function) 

this works pretty well except for the in-memory io manager because we don't have a way to access the instantiation of the in-mem io manager that is storing the output


I haven't had time to dig into this yet, but on the chance that addressing it causes the user facing API for input managers to shift a little bit, I think it should stay experimental.

Actually, I misspoke about the byte order mark. I updated my comment above.

The default `Encoding.UTF8` instance will emit a byte order mark when used with something like `System.IO.StreamWriter`, where the encoding's `GetPreamble()` is called.

If you're just calling `GetBytes` on the encoding, it won't include the byte order mark.

So we can simply do:

```c#
var encoded = Convert.ToBase64String(System.Text.Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes("haha business"));
```

I experimented with having `DynamicOut` yield sklearn model objects but decided against this. The reason is that it makes the particular set of hyperparameters you are optimizing less clear. In this example, there are more hyperparameters for both kinds of models than just the ones we are searching over (they're just set to defaults in the sklearn objects). If we directly yield model objects, then we later have to re-extract the particular hps we are varying to state the result of optimization. It is less complex IMO to simply keep them out as a separate entity. It's actually these little bundles of HP subsets (not the model objects) that are the domain we are optimizing over.

This was my thought as well. Back when I called it "launch_action", it was very clear that it was only intended for use in "launch". However simply saying "ignore" and "obsolete" is a log more generic of a name.

My thoughts on this matter are:
1. Personally, I like the idea that "ignore" and "obsolete" are only ever regarded by "launch" and "prune" actions (and I suppose diff as well). It's cleaner to implement that way and you can always override these by explicitly using `create`, `update`, and `delete`, which know nothing about these configurations.
2. If we were to make all commands care about these, it would be a LOT more work and would require implementation likely within SceptrePlan itself of filtering these things out rather than at the specific CLI command level. This is possible, of course, but it would then mean we'd either lose the ability to override with `create`, `update`, and `delete` or we'd need to have special arguments to override `ignore` and `obsolete`. Both of these seem like messier implementations.
3. I agree that the current names of `ignore` and `obsolete` are a bit deceptive, since it would stand to reason that `ignore` would be globally applied (as opposed to `launch_action`, which seems clearly related to `launch` only.
4. I think `ignore` is the more problematic of the two configs for the above reasons. `obsolete` is more easily understood as only applying to pruning actions.
5. Perhaps we could clarify the `ignore` config by renaming it `ignore_launch`? That seems very clear at what it means to me and doesn't seem like it has to be applied more broadly.

clients should assume that, if target did unlock the transfer, they've agreed they've received at least the amount intended (`secret.amount`), but then this must be validated strictly.
`payment_identifier` was also previously validated by initiators, and although it's just a metadata, it _may_ contain sensible information which we should ensure this is correct.

The original code has bug in it:

Let's assume that `aNeighbor` references to a `Router` instance in `mRouterTable`. 
- After `ProcessRouteTlv` is executed, `aNeighbor`  can point to a different `Router`, because `ProcessRouteTlv` can move Routers in `mRouterTable`. So, after `ProcessRouteTlv`, the `Router` instance referenced by `aNeighbor` is not what we expect it to be, the sender of the `MLE Advertisement`. So, any later handling of `aNeighbor` can be a potential bug. 
- If we are not lucky, `aNeighbor` now references to a `Router` instance with `RLOC16 == 0xFFFF` (Router ID not allocated). In such case, `RemoveNeighbor(*aNeighbor);` at the `exit` will be executed and the original `RemoveNeighbor` will treat this neighbor as a `Child` instance which it is not. 

One way of addressing it would be reassigning `aNeighbor` to the correct `Router`. Meanwhile assigning `aNeighbor` to `NULL` seems also OK to me according to analysis of the following code in this function.

**the problem**:

> We have `"${LIBR_HOME}/bin/${R_ARCH}"` in the set of paths to search, and `LIBR_CORE_LIBRARY` isn't being found...but when I set it directly it works.

When I say "directly", I mean going back to what I originally had

```
set (LIBR_CORE_LIBRARY ${LIBR_HOME}/bin/${R_ARCH}/R.dll CACHE PATH "R core shared library")
```

Let me show you:

* Just adding MinGW to PATH (plus other extra logging that I'll remove):
    - commit: https://github.com/jameslamb/LightGBM/pull/19/commits/05c59f48505c2c1231d3d5e1d2b9059a55602c02
    - AppVeyor: [works](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/jameslamb/lightgbm/builds/32280450/job/vk10elspubecgufx)

        - `Could NOT find LibR (missing: LIBR_CORE_LIBRARY)`
* Hardcoding path guess:
    -  commit: https://github.com/jameslamb/LightGBM/pull/19/commits/fdd57243537a125da0e0c176128f6e1beee9a043
    - AppVeyor: [works](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/jameslamb/lightgbm/builds/32280547)
        - you can see log message `LIBR_CORE_LIBRARYwas found by find_library()`
    - Azure: [works](https://dev.azure.com/JayLamb20/jameslamb-lightgbm/_build/results?buildId=391&view=logs&j=b51fa6ee-4364-59e6-4527-dc4aaafd2db2&t=85bde6ad-0953-589b-e1e8-633bb2d9d530)
        - you can see log message `LIBR_CORE_LIBRARY still not found, trying hard-coding`

From the `Get-ChildItem` calls and added cmake logs, I can see in the logs that these are the relevant paths

* AppVeyor
    - R.dll: `C:\projects\lightgbm\RLibrary\R\bin\x64\R.dll`
    - `LIBR_HOME`: `C:/projects/lightgbm/RLibrary/R`
* Azure
    - R.dll: `D:\a\1\s\RLibrary\R\bin\x64\R.dll`
    - `LIBR_HOME`: `D:/a/1/s/RLibrary/R`

So then I thought "ok, the paths are the same...maybe the `CMake` versions are different and `find_library()` behaves differently...they are! and it does!

* Appveyor: 3.16
    -  I can see `C:/Program Files (x86)/CMake/share/cmake-3.16` in the logs
* Azure: 3.17
    - following the [Microsoft docs on the 'vs2017-win2016' image we use](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/pipelines/agents/hosted?view=azure-devops) to [the list of installed software](https://github.com/actions/virtual-environments/blob/master/images/win/Windows2016-Readme.md#cmake)

I found an important-looking line in https://cmake.org/cmake/help/v3.17/release/3.17.html#id17

> When using MinGW tools, the find_library() command no longer finds .dll files by default. Instead, it expects .dll.a import libraries to be available.

I'm confused though, it seems like passing `R.dll`  to `NAMES` should work ([CMake docs](https://cmake.org/cmake/help/latest/command/find_library.html)):

> Each library name given to the NAMES option is first considered as a library file name and then considered with platform-specific prefixes (e.g. lib) and suffixes (e.g. .so). Therefore one may specify library file names such as libfoo.a directly. 

I'm going to try something like this:

```
# look for the core R library
find_library(
  LIBR_CORE_LIBRARY
  NAMES R R.dll
  HINTS "${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}" "${LIBR_HOME}/lib" "${LIBR_HOME}/bin/${R_ARCH}" "${LIBR_HOME}/bin" "${LIBR_LIBRARIES}"
)

if (WIN32 AND NOT LIBR_CORE_LIBRARY)
    find_file(
        LIBR_CORE_LIBRARY
        NAME R.dll
        HINTS "${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}" "${LIBR_HOME}/lib" "${LIBR_HOME}/bin/${R_ARCH}" "${LIBR_HOME}/bin" "${LIBR_LIBRARIES}"
    )
endif()
```

Right now the model frame that one can get with `model_frame = fm.model` has this kind of layout: two columns, names `z` and `n`, both `float64`. So there is no way to confuse these two sets of coefficients by looking at the frame content. Then there is a way to re-use the model with `fm.model = model_frame`, so I wanted to be as strict as possible and not load anything that just has two `float64` columns. Because the probability is higher that this frame is not a model frame, but something else. 

In principle, this could be replaced by some convention like `z` is the first column, and `n` is the second one, but then the probability is higher that some mess could be accepted as a model. Right now I just have the same requirements as to how the `model` frame should look like for both getter output and setter input.

Basically lexicon providers should support all possible ways to identify a record when doing a create, update or delete operation:
- relative (`name`)
- full (`name.example.com`) 
- FQDN (`name.example.com.`)

list operations will display the record in a [standardized format](https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon/blob/master/SPECIFICATION.md#general), using the full name.

All providers inherit from a `base` provider which has a number of helpful utility methods that will allow you to convert from one record type to another. 
https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon/blob/master/lexicon/providers/base.py#L94-L114

You can see how this data massaging is used in the [`cloudflare` provider](https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon/blob/master/lexicon/providers/cloudflare.py#L65) to take the user input and consistently convert it to a format that cloudflare's api expects.

> This specific rule will rerun every time, but the downstream rules will only rerun if the output of this specific rule changes.

This is correct. Uncacheable nodes are not completely free, but they are cheaper than actually re-running the logic above the uncacheable node.

An uncacheable node runs once per "session" (typically one pants run), and everything that depends on an uncacheable node is effectively always marked "dirty". Dirty nodes do not re-run unless their dependencies (recursively, all the way down to the uncacheable node) have changed output values, represented using an integer generation value. That rust-only recursion on integer graph ids and integer generation values is much cheaper than actually running the nodes.

BUT: two things.
1. It's possible that this patch will experience the kinds of issues that @gshuflin ran into on #10129 (...although we've been using uncacheable nodes for `test --force`, so perhaps not)
2. This might be better modeled (eventually?) as a native operation of the `CommandRunner`s as I suggested originally on #9760: see the followup ticket I created after #9760 landed: #10526.

When we match an arn to a regular expression, I thought it would be more clear to denote what resource type the ARN is supposed to match to. That way, we can more clearly detect when an ARN is a APIGW RestApi arn, or something else, by checking the `Resource` field of the matched object.

I get why this is confusing, because the information is really only used in the APIGW cases. I think a better refactor would be to just have a list of constants with clear names as the regular expressions.

Eg. `LAMBDA_FUNCTION_ARN_REGEX` = `....` and `APIGW_RESTAPI_REGEX`. Then, we when checking for a match, we can say "if the expression matched was `APIGW_RESTAPI_REGEX`, do this logic.

When it comes to the choice of stealing _half_ of the task (instead of a larger or smaller fraction of the total), the idea is that when a _thief_ initiates stealing, it has no tasks in flight. So by stealing half of the tasks, we make sure that _thief_ and _victim_ end up with the same number of tasks in flight. Of course, if tasks are very short, when the _thief_ receives the stolen tasks, the _victim_ might have less than half of the original workload (because it has executed some in the meantime).

So, FWIW: a (minor) consideration here is that both the `NodeKey` and the `NodeOutput` are kept in memory. Smaller inputs/outputs are better for memory usage (possibly also for performance if they're being copied), but might mean more work to get the value that will actually be used by a consumer.

As an example: not holding `Snapshot` in memory is a win from a memory usage perspective, because we will load the `Snapshot` from the LMDB store any time is used. That might take some time, but is very minor relative to re-capturing the `Snapshot`. On the other hand, `FileListing` is now being kept in memory, and I don't really see an alternative since there is no more useful key to identify it with (i.e. no equivalent of `Digest` for this case).

The lowercasing of sub-paths was prompted by the fact that resources are now in their own sub-pages, the fragments of section headers are lowercased, and CloudFront path patterns are case-sensitive. If a user was previously linking to the following resource section headers `/s3.html#service-resource` or `.../s3.html#bucketlifecycleconfiguration`, we would (without the lowercasing) want to reroute to `/s3/ServiceResource/index.html` which is mappable but we'd have to do the same for `/s3/BucketLifeCycleConfiguration/index.html` and all other resources (93 across 10 services). These are the options I see:
1. Leave as is and sacrifice casing.
2. Create a huge mapping of lowercased resource names to correctly cased names.
3. Don't support service resource section header redirections. Note: Resource class/method links will still be rerouted as normal since their fragments provide the correct casing.

There are 3 sources that can trigger `EvaluateRoutingPolicy`:
- Router solicit timer fired for the 3rd time
- RA received with PIO of an On-Link prefix
- Thread network data changed

None of these conditions can we guarantee that will happen in the future. 

My suggestion would be using a periodical timer to trigger `EvaluateRoutingPolicy` timely so that  any abnormality can be fixed eventually.

I did see that done elsewhere in the repo, and I'm happy to adhere to the current practice of relying on default initialization of `absl::optional<T>` in these kind of "return an unset optional" cases, but I do think there are a few advantages of returning `absl::nullopt` instead of `{}`:

1. I personally think that it's more readable, and that it more clearly conveys that we're returning an unset optional. It may be because my C++ is a bit rusty, but I had to think through the `{}` return to make sure that this default initialization did what I expected, while with the `absl::nullopt` return, the semantics were immediately clear to me.
2. There's been [some weirdness in the past](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/57964217/stdoptional-construct-empty-with-or-stdnullopt) where default initialization ends up value-initializing (i.e. zero-initializing) `optional` implementations that don't have a user-provided default constructor (i.e. the default constructor is implicitly declared or explicitly defaulted), causing unexpected overhead for something that people _thought_ was equivalent to the `absl::nullopt` constructor path. It was made worse by this happening in GCC but not Clang. This resulted in the recommendation of returning an explicit `absl::nullopt` instead, to avoid that ambiguity/weirdness.

(2) has been [fixed in GCC](https://github.com/gcc-mirror/gcc/commit/bafd12cb22e83b7da8946873513a897e48e2900f#diff-98ccb1166278152524d4e41464c3c17597ca7409fcee5756759f900359838dc5), Clang's implementation of `std::optional` has (I think) always had [a user-provided default constructor](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/9c978dd6e12e5ffaf5441f459db47e9892b09a82/libcxx/include/optional#L688), and I've checked the implementation of `absl::optional` and it [seems as if this isn't an issue](https://github.com/abseil/abseil-cpp/blob/c5c4db4f5191fe5e76cbf68dcc71fb28702f7d2b/absl/types/optional.h#L128-L130), but I still decided to go with `absl::nullopt` since it seemed more explicit and readable.

(2) aside, let me know if you think returning `absl::nullopt` is more readable than returning `{}`, and maybe we can port to that pattern in the future. In the meantime, I'll stick with returning `{}` like other uses of `absl::optional` in the repo. 😄

@nsheaps A follow up from our previous discussions about replacing this `try/except` block with checking the config for metrics being enabled:

It turns out there are two different ways to enable the current statsd metrics (and you cannot set both at the same time):  
1. `metrics.tagging` enables the tagged metrics (described [here](https://github.snooguts.net/reddit/baseplate.spec/blob/master/component-apis/metrics-tagged.md))
2. `metrics.namespace` enables non-tagged metrics (described [here](https://github.snooguts.net/reddit/baseplate.spec/blob/master/component-apis/metrics.md)).

Searching in sourcegraph, it looks like there are approx. 82 repos that set `metrics.namespace` and 20 repos that set `metric.tagging`.  For reference, here are the sourcegraph queries I ran:
```
repo:reddit-service-* repo:contains.file(requirements.txt) file:ini metrics.tagging select:repo
repo:reddit-service-* repo:contains.file(requirements.txt) file:ini metrics.namespace select:repo
```

Since the goal is to enable Prometheus metrics alongside the current statsd metrics (until wavefront is turned off), then we need to enable Prometheus when either `metrics.tagging` or `metrics.namespace` are set.

Another consideration is to create a new config option specifically for Prometheus.  It would be nice to have a separate config for the Prometheus metrics to keep it untangled from the current metric configs options, however there wouldn't be a way to turn on Prometheus automatically without updating the `.ini` file which would make upgrades not backwards compatible.  But I'm open to a discussion either way.

IMO mandating that the first request be empty feels like it unduly favors an uncommon case (resuming an partial upload where the client is unsure what progress it made) over the far more common case (where the entire blob is <4MB and we could otherwise upload the entire thing in a single request).

If we make that part optional, the client could still use that to check progress in cases where it knows it's resuming an upload or if it knows that it's using a blob sequence that already exists, but in the common case where it's just churning through a time series and for each step creating 1 blob sequence and 1 blob, I think that would basically get us a free reduction from 3 to 2 round trips per step.

One other consideration too is that if we had CreateBlobSequence/GetBlobSequence return the WriteBlobRequest properties (for the blob if it already exists) then the client could still do the check against sending redundant data, but as part of the first RPC rather than needing a second intermediary RPC.

We used to think it was convenient to set up a default handler at import time, but we have since learned differently. The Python logging documentation advises strongly against libraries adding handlers at import time. In practice it seems tog get in the way as often as it helps; it's better to just provide and advertise a convenience function for users to call to add special logging, and then set it up to suit at the application level.

For example, at NSLS-II, we plan to call set up handlers in the profiles, ensuring that log messages are always written to some standard location.

Regarding the reason why `FunctionNodeVisitor` doesn't work, I think that's because of the structure around this:

```py
 def visit(self, node): 
     super().visit(node)   #  <<<=== 1st visit (possibly `Attribute` won't be visited this time)
     self.annotation_visitor.visit(node) 
  
     attr_descendants = node.get_descendants(vy_ast.Attribute) 
     for attr_descendant in attr_descendants: 
         self.visit(attr_descendant)    # <<<=== 2nd visit (this is to make sure to visit at least all the `Attribute` nodes missed on 1st visit above)
```

The way visiting the `Attribute` on 2nd time doesn't setup namespace properly. For example, from CI,

https://github.com/vyperlang/vyper/runs/4707801642?check_suite_focus=true#step:5:6264

```py
for i in ...:
  ... _baz[i].x ...  # <<<=== try to visit this `Attribute` node
```

here, this tries to visit `Attribute` node `_baz[i].x` without setting up the namespace for the for-loop variable `i` and thus `get_exact_type_from_node` on the value node `_baz[i]` is failing.

I haven't actually debug it, so I might be wrong, but this is my intuition of the issue.

But while I was digging into it, I found another edge case:

1. Drag an item around and put the item back to its original position.
2. The Lit format is messed up by dragging around.
3. But the cleanup block (toggling `_renderEmptySortable)` won't be executed because putting the item back to its original position don't trigger `onSort` event.
4. Therefore the format won't be cleaned up and causing issues in other user interactions.

The bug:

![Kapture 2020-11-27 at 16 00 25](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7324708/100486498-b64d2180-30c9-11eb-9f80-c3c645d38a53.gif)

The fix I put in is that instead of observing `onSort` event, we observe `onEnd` event, which will be triggered even if an item is dragged and put back to its original position. In the block, we check on `evt.oldIndex !== evt.newIndex` to decide if a websocket message needs to be sent.

> Should ci.py have an option --integration-tests-v2? I think absolutely yes. This makes it easier to run ci.py to test specific things, like test just the integration tests vs unit tests. Notably, this script can have multiple options at a time so we could run something like ci.py --unit-tests --integration-tests-v2 --remote-execution-enabled.

IMO, ci.py should not be necessary to do anything, and actually running "everything that runs in CI" is generally not a good idea. If that looks like being able to emit the `./pants` command that _would_ have run so that someone can whittle it down to just the specific test that they want to run, then that would be my preference vs increasing the complexity of ci.py.

> Less confusing to CI users. The shard name "Unit tests and some remoted integration tests" isn't particularly clear to me.

I buy this from a clarity perspective. But with concurrency in play, the distinction between unit and integration is less relevant, and we might consider prioritizing tests by their timeouts/runtimes in the v2 runner (and starting longer running tests first) instead of actually tagging things integration/unit. Twitter does not use the integration/unit labels internally, and I'm not sure that Pants needs to either.

Checking indices for equality before joining will save us a decent time:
```python
import pandas
import numpy as np
from timeit import default_timer as timer


N = 50_000_000

ind1 = pandas.Index(np.arange(N))
ind2 = pandas.Index(np.arange(N))

t1 = timer()
if not ind1.equals(ind2):
    result = ind1.join(ind2, how="outer")
else:
    result = ind1
print("Checking for equality:", timer() - t1)  # ~ 0.1s

t1 = timer()
result = ind1.join(ind2, how="outer")
print("Do not checking for equality:", timer() - t1)  # ~ 8.3s
```

The PyPI package publishing is done automatically via GitHub actions when a new tag is pushed.

To manually trigger the release for new version, follow this process:

* Create a new branch with the name `release-VER` (ex release-20.1.0) No need to create a separate issue.
* On that branch update the version from `ldaptor/__init__.py` to the
  final version.
* Update the NEWS file with the final version and release date.
* Push the commit and record the commit SHA as this will be the one used to
  tag and do the release.
* Include the tagged commit to the PR description.
* On the same branch, update the `ldaptor/__init__.py` to a `.dev0` version
  and update NEWS to a new empty unreleased section.
* Push the latest commit and request a review for the PR.
* Once the PR is approved, you can merge the PR
* After the merge, push the release tag. GitHub action will take care of the release.

You can check the GitHub actions release process here https://github.com/twisted/ldaptor/actions

Below is the template text for the unreleased NEWS section::

    20.0.1 (unreleased)
    -------------------

    Changes
    - Nothing changed yet.

The above process can be automated by running::

    pipx run --spec="zest.releaser[recommended]>=6.22.1" fullrelease
```

But if there are more than one commands, we recommend do it like that doc suggested. 
> If a command subgroup would only have a single command, move it into the parent command group and hyphenate the name. This is common for commands which exist only to pull down cataloging information (e.g. database list-sku-definitions instead of database sku-definitions list). However, if you plan to add commands to this group in the future, then temporarily having a command group with a single command would make more sense. 

Such as:
```

> Can you please help explain why we need immediate eventing for OTNS to work (e.g., need to know exactly when RLOC16 or role value changes).

One of the motivation for OTNS to use status emitting to acquire node status rather than calling APIs is because the **node status affects the simulation result immediately**. 
For example, OTNS uses node `rloc16` and `extaddr` for destination address filtering so that it only dispatches unicast messages to the right destinations. This is one of the reasons that `OTNS` outperforms existing virtual time simulator by tens of times. 

Using `Tasklets` for event emitting changes the order of events, e.x. message transmission, extaddr/rloc16 change, etc.  If we are to implement MAC filters in OTNS, the order of message transmission events and `extaddr` change events could cause `OTNS` to incorrectly drop messages. 

In general, I am worried about the disordering of events caused by `posted Tasklets` because order of events impacts `OTNS` simulation result. 

> A higher level simulator can then use existing public APIs to query info/state from OT (e.g., check the current role using otThreadGetDeviceRole()). This can be done after every call that would change OT.

This however implies that status emitting to `OTNS` must be implemented in one of apps or platforms. However, I would expect `OTNS` to work for cross apps/platforms in the future. For example, with `OTNS` enhancements and proper `otPlatOtnsStatus` implementation on `nrf528xx` platform, we can even use `OTNS` for simulating or visualizing real devices. Integrating `OTNS` mechanisms in the OpenThread core equips `OTNS` with more potentials. 

> but if we allow and use OtnsStatus directly in the core modules, it may be used in places where there is no callback and adds dependency in the code.

I would expect this never to happen that we call `OtnsStatus` in the places where there is no need to call callbacks. `OtnsStatus` should be called whenever the target status changes, in which case, we should also call callbacks. If the target status does not change, we don't need to call `OtnsStatus` or callbacks. Replacing `OtnsStatus` with callbacks should have the exact same overhead of maintaining code dependency as core code changes. 

---
With all these concerns, I am not sure using registered callbacks is suitable for status emitting to `OTNS`. For now, it seems best and logical to me to integrate `OTNS` utilities to OT core and expect future improvements when we gain more experience.

Alternatively, we could somehow factor this `coordinator_of_binaries` rule out so that each call site can choose an error message that's meaningful to it? I'm not sure how trivial this would be.

Generally, I'd bias towards optimizing for the better error message and figuring out whatever gymnastics we need to support that great error message.

The issue is that the backend (both pyav and VideoReader) consume data in `pts` which is unfortunate as they are not standardised per stream (e.g. audio and video have different timebases so pts=24 means different things). In a PR some time ago, we've tried to fix this by introducing `sec` as a default unit that would then be converted to appropriate stream. This caused an imprecision issue (bc thigs would be incorrectly rounded) which broke all datasets bc VideoClips creation requires fairly precise pts and such rounding erros cause major issues. We've initially fixed these by using `pts` as a default in VideoClips (which raises this warrning). This would be fine (i.e. I'd keep the warning and try to re-work the video clips) if all else was equal, but the PR mentioned above was reverted for internal issues, so this warrning is not really relevant anymore. 

Having said that, I have a major PR with a VideoReading re-work in plan that will make this redundant in a way.

A related observation: While the tokenizer is linear, it searches across the regex patterns for the matching token, so **the lexical analysis runtime also depends on the type of tokens in the input.**

However, basic s-expressions can be tokenized with a single character look-ahead, and except for the tokens starting with a `'` the [Clarity lexical grammar](https://github.com/blockstack/blockstack-core/blob/7d162419f0bd77a4273a158a989669ee5f8fe9c0/src/vm/ast/parser/mod.rs#L76) is LL(2) allowing `0x` hexstrings and `u` for unsigned integers.

**This allows for a faster and more predictable tokenizer runtime** e.g by using an FSM with a O(1) lookup from character to token.

This section is something I added that you won't find in the `xgboost`  script. It solves an issue that only matters at testing time...basically inside of  `R CMD check`, CRAN actually runs a different command called `RD CMD  check` which uses a special version of `R` / `R.exe`.  We faced this [in pkgnet](https://github.com/uptake/pkgnet/pull/108/files#r230631169) as well.

From [Writing R Extensions](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-exts.html#Writing-portable-packages)

> Do not invoke R by plain R, Rscript or (on Windows) Rterm in your examples, tests, vignettes, makefiles or other scripts. As pointed out in several places earlier in this manual, use something like

>    "$(R_HOME)/bin/Rscript"
>    "$(R_HOME)/bin$(R_ARCH_BIN)/Rterm"

> with appropriate quotes (as, although not recommended, R_HOME can contain spaces).

The hints that I gave here will only work on Unix-alike operating systems. When I do #2335 it will get changed. But since this only matters for testing (in the context of `R CMD check`), not for normal use, I think it's ok to merge.

Without this block, the builds will fail with this error:

> 'R' should not be used without a path -- see par. 1.6 of the manual

without `-z`:  
`0    0    file1 => renamed file with spaces`
with `-z` that becomes:  
```
0    0
file1
renamed file with spaces```
so the `maxsplit=2` no longer protects from splitting filenames on spaces. Though this needs further modification to fix how it handles when files are renamed. I added a comment to the main thread here asking how to handle renaming of files in the history tab.

Another thing I learned from the typing expert team mate:

>> Would it be better to have lower-case names (tuple, dict, list, set) in the output?

> It depends on what Python versions you need to support. In Python 3.10 and higher, it is indeed preferred to use the lowercase builtin names. 

That makes me think `#ifdef`s of some sort would be best (to produce `Tuple` for <= 3.9 and `tuple` otherwise), although that adds a bit of clutter until we can drop 3.9 support.

Tying that back to my other comment, `py::typing::tuple` vs `py::typing::Tuple`:

Pure Python `typing.Tuple` will likely be deleted at some point in the future, although IIUC there is no timeline yet.

We cannot reuse `py::tuple` like pure Python reuses `tuple` for typing purposes.

Should we have `py::typing::tuple` or `py::typing::Tuple`? I don't know. It's on the awkward side either way.

I think I'd settle for `py::typing::tuple` (for any Python version), to not look old fashioned in the long run.

Ideally, yes. It'd involve defining a new analyzer gRPC method (e.g. `Package`) and then having the Policy SDK for each language provide an implementation.

For Python, this would mean porting the TGZ archiving implementation (with ignore behaviors) of this PR from Go to Python.

For Node.js, it'd mean calling `npm pack` from within Node.js Policy SDK. Though, we'd need to keep the existing Go implementation of shelling out to `npm pack` for previous versions of the Node.js Policy SDK where the `Package` gRPC method doesn't exist.

It can be fine to keep the precision of the input if the image is not too large, but these cumulative sums can become very inaccurate for large image sizes. The basic issue is that the cumulative sum may be very large and then you are doing addition of relatively small values to very large values which leads to inaccuracy. The same effect is still present in float64, but just won't be as noticeable until larger image sizes are reached. 

There was a concrete example using the integral images implemented internally to non-local means ([see here](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/2878#issuecomment-346344773) and in the subsequent comment). In that case the error is highly visible in the image for float32 when the input is shape 600x1000x1000, but not visually noticeable for float64.

One compromise to avoid a behavior change would be to do the cumsum in float64, but then cast the final sum to the dtype of the input?

Minor nit: this sounds like the xblock expects a _single_ nested `<provider>` tag, which is then interpreted as a list. I'd suggest to rephrase this as:

```markdown
* `provider`: Reference to an OpenStack provider configured in the platform.
  It `name` attribute must match one of the providers in the XBlock configuration,
  `capacity` specifies how many environments should be launched in that
  provider at maximum (where 0 means keep launching environments until 
  encountering a launch failure), and 
  `environment` specifies a content store path to a Heat environment file specifying
  template parameters for that provider.
  This element must be specified at least once, and can be specified multiple times
  to use one or more fallback providers.
```

In the tables above, it was the change from 14.4ms to 11.3ms.  It's equivalent, and clearer if we go to the original protocol-buffer [reference](https://developers.google.com/protocol-buffers/docs/encoding?csw=1#types) in the spec that says

> `(n << 1) ^ (n >> 63)`
> Note that the second shift – the (n >> 31) part – is an arithmetic shift. So, in other words, the result of the shift is either a number that is all zero bits (if n is positive) or all one bits (if n is negative).

In other words
- xor with all zeros if it's positive, so do nothing, or
- xor with all ones if it's negative, so a not-operator.

I'm assuming cython does two pythonic shifts, which appears to be more expensive than one comparison and one shift.

That said, I committed a version that keeps the original formula, but enforces the datum to be a long.  This makes it even more efficient (~9ms for that test), but I was reluctant to do so as I wasn't sure about python 2.7.

I also added a test to verify the extreme values, and compared encoding offline with avro by adding these lines to `timeit_benchmark`:
```
    list_long_record = [-2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 2147483647, -2147483648,
                        9223372036854775807, -9223372036854775808]

    test_case("Compare long encoding", array_schema,

```

This is how instructGPT introduces RLHF. the truth is that you don't get any evaluation signal until the end of the sentence. i.e. the reward model is queried only when the answer is complete to evaluate whether the response was good for the input instruction. So your discount factor should be 1 here because you want to propagate that final reward equally to every token decision you have made so far. Also this way you'll only do one forward pass on RM and b/c you don't need to call it auto-regressively. However if it's a conversation things differ, the model can ask for clarifications etc. but in conversation you also have to generate full sentences on each turn. If you incorporate RLHF in conversation fashion, then your model can learn to ask you clarifying questions, because that will help it give more satisfying answers faster.

It seemed reasonable to have P/Cs for simple serdes (which furthermore allowed for beautiful terse inline specification of them), which was applicable to most scenarios and defer avro functionality to elsewhere. Initially, i was thinking this could even be done with C# extension methods, not requiring a the classes for avro (but that was not the case). But it turned out to work much better to have completely general serde delegate types (two of them, one async, one not) and have the structure @rnpridgeon is suggesting above. @rnpridgeon's comment about avro being too specific also re-enforces this choice.

Yes, on the Python side (e.g. in `Connection`) we could choose to implement a write buffer and "continue where we left off". As [I mentioned](https://github.com/redis/redis-py/issues/2499#issuecomment-1346737191) before, the problem with a write buffer would be the potential of flushing out a command that's stale from an app code's perspective.

What I'd rather see, is being able to, instead of flushing a command that can be stale, to send a short control sequence that would cancel a command that's half-written into the socket, turning it into a no-op. This is the feature that RESP protocol doesn't support.

So:
1. Write buffer: Not an inherent limitation in Python, we simply don't want to do it because it's bad UX.
2. Cancel a half-written command: Inherent limitation of RESP.

Hence the "problem" is RESP, not `send_packed_command`.

Hi, this question is not about the rank of the sparse-tensor: we do permit sparse-tensors of arbitrary rank. The question is about whether the value stored for a given sparse-index can be, say, N floats instead of a single float. Pytorch, for example, allows this. It allows us to take a d-dimensional tensor, and say that the first s-dimensions are sparse and the remaining (d-s) dimensions are dense. In this case, the values field becomes a (d-s)-dimensional tensor, while the indices becomes a [NNZ, s] dimensional tensor. Representation-wise, this is easy enough to support. However, the implementation of ops will be somewhat more complex to support this generality (vs. treating each value as a scalar).

About your comment on calculating the subset, you just have been triggered doing a "do not repeat yourself" reasoning on Windows ^^

Yes, this would work, to just define in a ACL the missing rights from a wider scoped ACL (on a group). But this is not a Windows style. A file or directory created by Windows will have by default one ACL on the user, on ACL for its primary group, independently of what each ACL contains.

So I prefer to stick with the way to do in Windows and stay consistent towards the platform.

On top of that, I think in general that it is not a good approach to rely on implicit resolution of rights between different scoped ACL. Either:
* you have only a global ACL (for your group) that define your rights
* or you have a specific ACL (for the user) on top of the global one, and then you redefine all the rights on the specific ACL.

The users don't need to set env vars anymore, and we don't need to parse env vars actually (in this setup).

Here's some context: 
- The only way to modify  the behavior of docker-compose files is to use environment variables.
- In the earlier version of the script (written in bash), instead of trying to parse many command line arguments and then issue the docker-compose commands with env variables initialized from those, I just passed on the user's current env vars to the command. As a result, the user had to set every important env variable.
- In the python script, the env vars that are applied to the compose commands and the env vars of the user's computer are completely separate. Thanks to the subprocess library, I construct the set of necessary variables for each compose command.
- As a result, it doesn't really matter what env variables are set on the host machine, we can reconstruct all the necessary env variables from the command line arguments of the script before calling the compose command.
- For backwards compatibility, I initially tried to write the python script such that its arguments can be set from environment variables AND command line arguments.
- The precedence there is CLI argument > env var > default.
- What that means is that all arguments have some default which is used if that argument is not set on the command line or the environment. If there's an env var but no CLI argument, the env var is used, if there's an env var and also a command line argument, the command line argument overwrites the value from the env var.
- To do this 3-way merging of arguments (between defaults, env vars and CLI args) I had to do all the custom stuff with mappings (from env var name to arg name) and suppressing the default `default` behavior of argparse (which doesn't play nicely with using a custom Namespace that can have values set at other points).

Our options:
1. Get rid of env var parsing for arguments: We can tell users to just pass in command line arguments for any configuration and get rid of all that messy logic and mappings. I've already rewrote the travis script to use CLI args for testing, we can just move everything to use the CLI args and tell people that's how you configure the codalab instance.

Pros:
    - Get rid of messy argument parsing logic
Cons:
    - If you have some local defaults you want to persist,  there's no way to do that and you need to use a long command with many arguments every time you do something

2. Instead of env vars, use some config file parsing (like JSON).

Pros:
    - Reduce messy merging logic (no more need for mapping env var names to arg names etc)
    - Allow people to save local defaults for easily redeploying etc
Cons:
    - Most of the messy logic still there (since we still need to 3-way merge CLI, JSON and default values)

3. Keep it like this with env vars being the way to persist your defaults

Pros:
    - Local defaults possible
    - No rewriting code portions
Cons:
    - No reduction in mess
    - Env vars are more opaque and may change since last time service started etc. So not great for rerunning etc.


Looking at this, option (2) seems to be the best option, but we can also go with (1) and tell people to save the long commands they use to start the service.

this has to do with the fact that the `SchedulingKeyEntry::StealableTasks() `function (https://github.com/gabrieleoliaro/ray/blob/atomic_work_stealing/src/ray/core_worker/transport/direct_task_transport.h#L324) that we use to quickly determine if there are stealable tasks is fast but not necessarily 100% correct all the time. For instance, if there are 10 active workers, and 5 tasks, all of which are queued at one worker, it would be beneficial for the remaining 9 workers to steal from the one that is overloaded, but since the number of tasks in flight (5) is less than the number of active workers (10), this won't happen. In the specific case of the test, we have 2 tasks and two active workers, so `total_tasks_in_flight `is NOT > `active_workers.size()`, and thus `SchedulingKeyEntry::StealableTasks()` returns false, resulting in one of the two workers being returned early.

This is kind of involved and interesting so let me break this down a bit.

The json files found in the channel folder are what coursier calls [app descriptors](https://get-coursier.io/docs/2.0.0-RC6-10/cli-install.html#application-descriptor-reference). An app descriptor is a json configuration file that describes how to pull in artifacts from maven repositories, which maven repositories to pull these artifacts from, java/scala/graal compiler options to build these artifacts with and what compiler to use to produce the executable artifacts. In a sense, these app descriptors are to their built on disk artifacts what docker files are to their built docker images.

Coursier channels are basically pointers to repositories of app descriptors. There exist a [default channel](https://github.com/coursier/apps/tree/master/apps/resources) but it contains fairly plain app descriptors. If you want to do something more fancy like compile `scalafmt` to a native image binary you're going to have to create a custom channel somewhere (gh repo, on disk folder or a web hosted json file), add your custom app descriptor there and point coursier at that channel.

This coursier integration assumes each repo a coursier hook lives in, to have a repo local channel folder specifically for pre-commit. It then attempts to build + locally install all the apps defined in the repo local pre-commit channel. Then the hook repo can export multiple pre-commit hooks pointing at different locally built coursier binaries with varying default arguments.

Similar how there is a docker + docker-image integration, I can see this being fast followed with an additional coursier integration that just points at remote hosted channels (other gh repos or web hosted json files) and imports artifacts from those.

The goal of this integration is to build executables from locally specified app descriptors though.

For the `_tensor_schema` parameter, we have a few options:

1. Arrow-agnostic dict of column name --> NumPy dtype and shape tuple: `_tensor_schema = {"f2": (np.float32, (2, 30, 30))}`
2. Arrow-aware dict of column name --> Arrow dtype and shape tuple: `_tensor_schema = {"f2": (pa.float32(), (2, 30, 30))}`
3. Arrow schema using our extension type: `_tensor_schema = pa.schema([("f2", ArrowTensorType(pa.float32(), (2, 30 30)))])`

(1) requires no knowledge of Arrow and probably provides the best UX overall, but I think that (3) aligns best with an Arrow-centric API, which might be what we want for Parquet if we're going to expose e.g. pushdown filter expressions, and aligns best with our current implementation (doing it manually via a cast in the read task) and future implementation (doing it automatically via a custom casting kernel for our extension type).

If we're planning on iterating on this anyway, I'd say that we should go with (1) first since it provides the best UX, and we can reexamine later if our Parquet reading API gets more Arrow-centric, or once we're looking at stabilizing this API.

We force loading to happen on the CPU because this script is supposed to work on machines without GPU as well.

In the (unlikely) case someone has a checkpoint from the internet which was saved on CUDA, and wants to do some training on the CPU (because they don't have GPU), the loading code would fail.
This makes sure that we are safe in this situation.

Note that loading the `checkpoint` on the CPU is always safe, because `load_state_dict` can perform inter-device copies, so the model can be on the GPU and the state-dict on the CPU

Another related idiom in Python is `try-except-else`:

```
try:
  run_command_that_may_raise_an_exception()
except:
  handle_error()
else:  # if there was no exception
  run_the_rest_of_the_code()
```

Usually the `try` block has only 1-2 lines and most of the logic goes into `else`. This makes it really explicit what code might be raising the error.

1. `objectTypes` actually isn't required, if it is `null` then it is assumed that all object types should be used. That said, I've changed the api to
```python
        public void Get2DSemanticHulls(
            List<string> objectIds = null,
            List<string> objectTypes = null
        ) {
```
so that a user can specify ids as well.

2. The main reason to have `objectTypes` is that this function is targeting semantic information which is inherently object-type-centric. It's "easy" to handle this all on the python side but is also a bit annoying in practice since you have to do it every time you call the function (or at least I would).

3. This overload is now in.

I've also added a comment:
```python
        /*
        For each objectId, create a convex hull of the object from a top-down view.
        The convex hull will be represented as a list of (x, z) world coordinates
        such that the boundary formed by these coordinates forms the convex hull
        of these points (smallest convex region enclosing the object's points).

        If the objectIds (or objectTypes) parameter is non-null, then only objects with
        those ids (or types) will be returned.
        
        ONLY ONE OF objectIds OR objectTypes IS ALLOWED TO BE NON-NULL.

        Returns a dictionary mapping object ids to a list of (x,z) coordinates corresponding
        to the convex hull of the corresponding object.
        */
```

    graph.bind("", EGNSSUB_V0)
    check_ns(graph, {"": EGNSSUB_V0})

    graph.bind("", None)

    assert list(graph.namespaces()) == [
        ("", URIRef("http://example.com/sub/v0")),
        ("default1", URIRef("None")),
    ]


def test_extant_store_unbind(tmp_path: Path, store_name: str) -> None:
    graph = make_graph(tmp_path, store_name)
    graph.bind("", EGNSSUB_V0)
    check_ns(graph, {"": EGNSSUB_V0})

    graph.store.bind("", None)

    if store_name == "BerkeleyDB":
        assert list(graph.store.namespaces()) == [
            ("", URIRef("http://example.com/sub/v0"))
        ]
    else:
        assert list(graph.store.namespaces()) == [("", None)]

I managed to vectorize the inversion just using numpy (and horner2d).  horner2d also proved faster than the previous forward-direction SIP/PV code that was there.  So now forward direction is ~10% faster and reverse is only ~3-5x slower instead of 50-100x.  

The numpy inversion is a bit suboptimal in that if just one coordinate requires an extra iteration, then the Newton-Raphson update is repeated for every coordinate.  We could probably chunk the input vector up to mitigate that if desired (or move things back down to c++), but I didn't bother.

This function is part of the user-facing public API of this module. As such, it needs quite a bit more detail in its docstring than this — otherwise, as a user, I probably wouldn't know what problem it solves for me, when it is appropriate to use it, and what functionality it replaces. That would mean it probably wouldn't get used, since users don't use features they don't understand or recognize the need for.

One especially important thing that we need to consider explicitly is this: how would a user know if _their_ introspection query is the same one that this module knows how to compute quickly? If the user is in-process and is therefore using `graphql-core` of the same version as the compiler, that's fine — but users don't have to do that. What if the user is using e.g. GraphiQL, which decides to send an introspection query to a server that is running this code? How would the server know if it can use this code to speed up the introspection or not? I don't think the current API solves this problem that users are very likely to run into, and I'd encourage you to revisit the end-to-end user experience and usage of this code in more detail.

However I do believe that the way _read_until runs in an endless loop is problematic given the way python run threads sequentially based on the GIL. Paramiko, which put itself in another thread using the threading library, has no chance receiving data if it's thread never gets scheduled by the python interpreter. In such endless loop waiting for another thread to do something, even a .00001 sleep gives more opportunities to python to schedule the another thread to get it's thing done.

I definitely don't love this idiosyncratic dict approach, but I think it is much easier to work with non-generically, and only slightly harder to work with generically.

For instance, with the demo from the PR description, where an image is tagged with `pants-{pants.hash}` and pushed to `@demo-reg` and I want a script to pull out the name (e.g. for putting into a cloud template), this might look like (sorry for the detail, I'm spelling it out for my benefit too):

Shell (via https://stedolan.github.io/jq/):

```shell
# dict
jq -r '.registries["@demo-reg"].tags["pants-{pants.hash}"].name' docker.docker-info.json

# list
jq -r '.registries[] | select(.alias == "demo-reg") | .tags[] | select(.template == "pants-{pants.hash}") | .name' docker.docker-info.json
```
Shell (via https://github.com/tomnomnom/gron):
```shell
# dict
gron docker.docker-info.json | grep '@demo-reg.*pants-{pants\.hash}.*name' | gron --values

# list: much harder
```

Python:

```python
# dict
data = json.load(open(...))
name = data["registries"]["@demo-reg"]["tags"]["pants-{pants.hash}"]["name"]

# list
data = json.load(open(...))
# (or with some sort of helper)
demo_reg = next(r for r in data["registries"] if r["alias"] == "demo-reg")
pants_tag = next(t for t in demo_reg["tags"] if t["template"] == "pants-{pants.hash}")
name = pants_tag["name"]
```

JS/TS:

```javascript
// dict
const data = JSON.parse(...)
const name = data.registries["@demo-reg"].tags["pants-{pants.hash}"].name

// list 
const data = JSON.parse(...)
const demoReg = data.registries.find(r => r.alias == "demo-reg")
const pantsTag = demoReg.tags.find(t => t.template == "pants-{pants.hash}")
const name = pantsTag.name
```

Handling the dict approach generically seems like it would swap (in Python syntax) `[handle(nested) for nested in parent]` to `{key: handle(nested) for key, nested in parent.items()}` which is annoying, but seems okay.

None of the examples above are catastrophically bad, so I'm happy to switch if you strongly prefer the more obvious/conventional list style.

Release tests can be launched either automatically (I) or through PR (II). 

For II, there are times that these two commits are intended to be different. On one hand, I want to use my own repository and my own PR commit for testing code. On the other hand, the wheel is still the latest wheel on master. This is useful when people update some test code and does not change source code. 

For the racing condition we are trying to address, it mostly applies to II.

For II, sequence of things that happen:
0. Latest wheel on master is wheel_n
1. a PR (that modifies both the test code and the source code) is landed.
2. a wheel is being built (let's call it wheel_n+1)
3. Now nightly release test starts to run.
4. in `run_release_test.sh`, we fetch most recent wheel and install it. This is wheel_n, since wheel_n+1 is still being built.
5. in `run_release_tst.sh`, we check out test code, which is current master (includes PR_n+1).
6. Now there is a discrepancy between source code (wheel version) and test code.

It seems to me that the way to resolve this (most reliably) is to deduce commit hash directly from the wheel installed. 

For the clone step in `build_pipeline.py`, are you referring to 
```
clone_cmd = f"git clone --depth 1 --branch {branch} {repo} {tmpdir}"
```
?

Let's say the branch and repo is ray master. 
This clone may still be subject to inconsistency right? No one guarantees that this clone will have the same head commit hash as the ray_wheel right? So it shouldn't be used as the source of truth.

This default value is what's causing most of these failing tests. AWS has a number of [different ways to provide credentials](https://boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/guide/credentials.html#configuring-credentials). By always passing a profile, we're forcing the credentials lookup to short circuit at step 4 and expect that there's a profile defined as `default`.

These buildkite hosts don't have a `~/.aws/credentials` file; consequently it can't find a `default` profile defined in that file. Hence all the `botocore.exceptions.ProfileNotFound: The config profile (default) could not be found` errors.

I think we should instead treat this more like we treat `endpoint_url` or `region_name` - completely optional and no default (which will cause it to default to `None`). That way, when we call `boto3.session.Session(profile_name=profile_name)`, it'll succeed regardless of whether a profile name is passed or not.

I suspect once you make that update, you'll see the overwhelming majority of these tests start passing.

What's the goal of a test like this? You're testing that the code is exactly what's written. If we somehow add support for running on Darwin, or if we switch to a different way of checking the platform instead of `sys.platform`, we just have some extra work to change or discard the test as well.

Rather than trying to execute every line of code in the tests, I'd suggest we focus on the predictive value of the tests. If a test fails, what's the chance that it's identifying a real bug in the code under test? What's the chance we need to change the test to match changes in the code? If the second is much more likely than the first (like in this test, as far as I can see), it's a waste of time.

If it's important to get "100% coverage", I'd prefer to [exclude sections from coverage](https://coverage.readthedocs.io/en/v4.5.x/excluding.html) instead of writing hyper-specific tests just to make those lines run.

I think this is exactly why we can't just use visibility points. The plant leaves for example, have no collision and do have visibility points scattered about the leaves, but the vis points arent enough to encompass all of where the leaf meshes actually are. What does encompass this are the trigger colliders that make up the leaves. If we only accounted for vis points for things like the plant, we could end up with a case where parts of the plant leaf mesh would be ignored in collision checks due to them not being included in the bounding box, and have bits of leaf clipping through a cabinet door or a wall or something.

It seems to me if the goal is to exclude receptacle areas from being included in the bounds generation, we should just exclude those receptacle trigger boxes explicitly and assume all other trigger colliders may be used to represent parts of a visible mesh. This seems not very intuitive but due to the way the triggers of specifically receptacle trigger boxes are used, I think it still makes sense.

Actually, _I think_ this lint caught an actual bug here, but I'm going to double-check. Here's my thinking.

This is my understanding of how the program is meant to function:

1. We load the `./run` module and call the `run` function inside of that module.
2. That function loads the user program and executes it.
3. Once the `run` function completes, we set `programRunning = false` on line 123. I would expect `programRunning` to always be false after the program has terminated.

The way this code _was_ written, you could call `programStarted()` after the program had already exited and you'd set `programRunning` to true again.  I don't think it's possible to have the program halt and then start back up again in the same invocation of the Language runtime (i.e. this `index.ts` file).

Suppose after the program has completed, another thread calls `programStarted()` to determine whether or not the program has started. `programStarted()` will return true and also update `programRunning` to true, without running the program again.


However, I think Kyle's question is still largely unanswered:

> Can you explain why programStarted() exists as a parameter before merging this? 🙂

Is `programStarted()` a callback intended to signal that `programRunning` should be set to true? If so, why does it return a boolean? It should be called `startProgram`. In this case, the change I made is incorrect for the reasons you've both noted. 😸 

Or is `programStarted()` intended to assay whether the program _was_ started? In this case, the lint has identified a bug. 🐛 The author probably intended to use `===` or `==` instead of `=`.

This part of the documentation is no longer true; these paragraphs can just be dropped. If you want to mention that Python iterables (including dicts) are interpreted with #ak.from_iter, then that would be good.

For reference, here is the old, removed behavior:

```python
>>> import awkward as ak
>>> array = ak.Array([[1, 2, 3], [], [4, 5]])
>>> builder = ak.ArrayBuilder()
>>> builder.append(array)

>>> builder.snapshot()
<Array [[[1, 2, 3], [], [4, 5]]] type='1 * var * var * int64'>

>>> builder.snapshot().layout
<ListOffsetArray64>
    <offsets><Index64 i="[0 3]" offset="0" length="2" at="0x55ceeb42f9f0"/></offsets>
    <content><IndexedArray64>
        <index><Index64 i="[0 1 2]" offset="0" length="3" at="0x55ceeb847d60"/></index>
        <content><ListOffsetArray64>
            <offsets><Index64 i="[0 3 3 5]" offset="0" length="4" at="0x55ceeb117a30"/></offsets>
            <content><NumpyArray format="l" shape="5" data="1 2 3 4 5" at="0x55ceeb1143d0"/></content>
        </ListOffsetArray64></content>
    </IndexedArray64></content>
</ListOffsetArray64>
```

and here is the new behavior:

```python
>>> import awkward as ak
>>> array = ak.Array([[1, 2, 3], [], [4, 5]])
>>> builder = ak.ArrayBuilder()
>>> builder.append(array)

>>> builder.snapshot()
<Array [[[1, 2, 3], [], [4, 5]]] type='1 * var * var * int64'>

>>> builder.snapshot().layout
<ListOffsetArray64>
    <offsets><Index64 i="[0 3]" offset="0" length="2" at="0x56492e998cb0"/></offsets>
    <content><ListOffsetArray64>
        <offsets><Index64 i="[0 3 3 5]" offset="0" length="4" at="0x56492efd8e20"/></offsets>
        <content><NumpyArray format="l" shape="5" data="1 2 3 4 5" at="0x56492efd8f20"/></content>
    </ListOffsetArray64></content>
</ListOffsetArray64>
```

The difference is that there's no longer any link to the original array (through an IndexedArray); the `builder.append(array)` treats the `array` like any Python iterable and copies its data.

> When you replace cloudpickle.CloudPickler with pickle.Pickler in my example you receive the desired behavior

Your example here works without defining `persistent_id` and `persistent_load`, so I am not sure it is relevant to why your changes are "abusing" the `persistent_id` protocol.


```python
In [9]: import io
   ...: import pickle
   ...:
   ...: __globals__ = {"a": "foo"}
   ...:
   ...:
   ...: class Pickler(pickle.Pickler):
   ...:     pass
   ...:
   ...: class Unpickler(pickle.Unpickler):
   ...:     pass
   ...:
   ...: exec("""
   ...: def get():
   ...:     return a
   ...: """, __globals__)
   ...: get = __globals__['get']
   ...: file = io.BytesIO()
   ...: Pickler(file).dump(get)
   ...: dumped = file.getvalue()
   ...: if b'foo' in dumped:
   ...:     raise Exception(repr(dumped))
   ...: get = Unpickler(io.BytesIO(dumped)).load()
   ...: if id(__globals__) != id(get.__globals__):
   ...:     raise Exception(f"id(__globals__) != id(get.__globals__)")
   ...: print(f"get() == {get()}")
   ...: __globals__['a'] = 'bar'
   ...: print(f"get() == {get()}")
```


I'll try to explain one more time why your PR does not use `persistent_id` as it is indented to be used. According to the `pickle` documentation:

> To pickle objects that have an external persistent ID, the pickler must have a custom persistent_id() method that takes an object as an argument and returns either None or the persistent ID for that object. When None is returned, the pickler simply pickles the object as normal. When a persistent ID string is returned, the pickler will pickle that object, along with a marker so that the unpickler will recognize it as a persistent ID.

In other words, `persistent_id(obj)` only has an impact on which `obj` is pickled. In your PR, `persistent_id(func.__globals__)` has an impact on the content of two different objects that are NOT `func.__globals__`: `f_globals` and `base_globals`. Such a case is never advertised by the `pickle` docs, and thus is an abuse of the `persistent_id` protocol.
